nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral-rich tropical reef flats and outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 2–25 m, typically in areas of dense coral growth. Wild specimens are commonly observed in pairs.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for grazing and refuge

diet.notes: In the wild, this species has been reported to feed mainly on coral polyps, making it what is sometimes called a partial obligate corallivore — an animal that relies significantly on live coral tissue as a food source — and this dietary habit contributes to its reputation for being difficult to wean onto captive foods. In captivity, it generally accepts a varied diet of meaty preparations (mysis shrimp, finely chopped crustacean flesh, frozen marine mixes) alongside algae-based and sponge-inclusive foods offered several times daily. Newly imported specimens may initially refuse prepared foods and have been reported to benefit from live mysis or feeding-stimulant preparations during the weaning period.

goodWith: other peaceful, similarly sized fish-only community species
avoidWith: other Chaetodon butterflyfishes in small tanks
ornamental shrimp and other delicate invertebrates
tankmates with similar coloration in undersized systems

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5986x3991. Tack-sharp clean lateral profile; vibrant orange-yellow body with diagnostic diagonal chevron stripes, black raccoon mask + white band and black caudal-peduncle bar all clearly shown. Best field-guide view.
Alan Slater, 1460x1080. Sharp in-situ Red Sea reef shot of whole fish in natural coral habitat; shows diagonal striping, mask and caudal bar. Adds natural-habitat context.
Hatem Moushir, 4928x3264. Sharp high-res aquarium view of a full, vibrant specimen; diagnostic diagonal stripes, raccoon mask and white band clear. Represents the in-tank hobbyist look.

summary: The Red Sea Raccoon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon fasciatus) is a Red Sea endemic closely related to the more widespread Raccoon Butterflyfish (C. lunula) and is generally considered more challenging to wean onto prepared foods. It is not reef-safe and is best suited to a fish-only system with experienced care.
careNotes: The Red Sea Raccoon Butterflyfish is endemic to the Red Sea and the Gulf of Aden and is closely related to the more widespread Raccoon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon lunula); the two are often confused in the trade. Adults reach roughly 18–22 cm and are active, wide-ranging swimmers, so a mature aquarium with ample open swimming space and live rock for grazing and refuge is generally advised — around 500 L is recommended given the species' adult size, tendency toward territorial behavior once acclimated, and its partial reliance on coral polyps as a natural food source. The species is generally considered peaceful toward other fish, but individuals have been reported to become territorial as adults, particularly toward others of the same species, other Chaetodon butterflyfishes, and tankmates with similar coloration competing for food. The species is best kept singly or as a verified mated pair, with multiples only attempted in much larger systems. Like other Chaetodon butterflyfishes, this species is commonly considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and lateral line — when kept in poor water quality or fed an insufficiently varied, vitamin-rich, algae-inclusive diet. Wild populations are reported as monogamous, so established pairs should not be split. Newly acquired specimens often refuse prepared foods for several days; targeted feeding with live or vitamin-enriched frozen mysis has been reported to help.
breedingNotes: Chaetodon fasciatus is oviparous and reported to form monogamous pairs, with the pair bond described as both obligate and socially maintained in wild populations. Captive breeding of Chaetodon butterflyfishes is generally considered impractical for hobbyists due to the difficulty of rearing pelagic larvae — the free-floating larval stage that requires highly specialized conditions — and no widely reported captive-bred lines exist for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (125 gal / ~473 L), temperature (72–78 °F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), temperament, reef-safe status, and diet guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), max length (22.0 cm TL, common 15 cm), native range (Red Sea endemic, reported from Gulf of Aden), depth (2–25 m), wild temperature range (24.7–29.2 °C, mean 27.2 °C), corallivorous diet, oviparous + monogamous reproduction.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Third source confirming minimum 125 gal tank, peaceful-but-territorially-defensive temperament once acclimated, not reef-safe (picks at corals and crustaceans), and feeding regime of meaty and algae-based foods multiple times daily.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Fourth source listing a lower minimum tank size (75 gal / ~284 L) and an intermediate care level; also confirms the 'Diagonal Butterflyfish' common name. The lower tank-size estimate is included in the averaging spread; the final 500 L recommendation biases toward the cautious larger value given adult size and territoriality.
